items.question_text: Use FIG 6-1-1. In a terminal adjacent-airport arrival case, a Category B-I aircraft will cross the flight path of a preceding Category A aircraft requiring wake turbulence separation. What minimum interval applies?
items.explanation: FIG 6-1-1 illustrates the adjacent-airport arrival application. For Category B-I behind Category A in terminal operations, the rule requires 3 minutes.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-1-2. In an adjacent-airport departure case, a small aircraft departs behind a B757 and will cross the B757's flight path. What en route wake turbulence interval applies?
items.explanation: FIG 6-1-2 illustrates the adjacent-airport departure application. In the en route rule, a small aircraft behind a B757 requires 2 minutes when crossing the preceding aircraft's flight path.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-1-3. In terminal operations, a Category H/I aircraft will land behind an arriving Category A aircraft on the same runway. What minimum interval is required?
items.explanation: FIG 6-1-3 supports the same-runway arrival wake application. Category H/I behind Category A requires 4 minutes in terminal operations.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-1-3. In en route operations, a small aircraft lands behind a heavy aircraft on the same runway. What arrival wake interval applies?
items.explanation: FIG 6-1-3 is the arrival-minima figure. For en route same-runway arrivals, a small aircraft behind a heavy aircraft requires 3 minutes.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-6. Two aircraft use intersecting runways. The runways diverge by 30 degrees or more. When may the succeeding aircraft be released under the depicted nonradar departure rule?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-6 supports the intersecting-runway case with 30-degree-or-greater divergence. The succeeding aircraft may take off when the preceding aircraft has passed the runway intersection.

choices.text: Only after radar identification is established.
When the preceding aircraft has passed the runway intersection.
When the preceding aircraft is 3 miles from the airport.
Only after the preceding aircraft begins a turn.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-8. A following departure will climb through the altitude assigned to a leading aircraft on the same course. When applying the timed version of the rule, what minimum applies until the following aircraft passes through the leading aircraft's altitude?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-8 illustrates the same-course climb-through timing rule. Without using the DME/ATD alternative, maintain at least 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the leading aircraft's altitude.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-2-9. Under the DME/ATD alternative for a following aircraft climbing through the leading aircraft's assigned altitude, what spacing is used?
items.explanation: FIG 6-2-9 depicts the DME/ATD alternative for same-course departure climb-through separation: use 5 miles between properly equipped aircraft under the stated DME/ATD conditions.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-3-1. If there is no appropriate approach fix and the departure takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course, what timing protects the arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: FIG 6-3-1 covers the no-fix timing case. When the takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees, the departure must take off at least 3 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport.

choices.text: The departure takes off at least 3 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport.
The departure takes off only after the arrival cancels IFR.
The departure takes off at least 5 minutes after the arrival passes the FAF.
The departure takes off when both aircraft are 5 miles apart by DME.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-3-2. In the no-fix divergent-course case, what must be true before the arrival is estimated at the airport?
items.explanation: FIG 6-3-2 illustrates the divergent-course timing rule. The departure must be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees at least 5 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport.

choices.text: The departure must be radar identified within 1 mile.
The arrival must be cleared for a visual approach.
The departure must be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees at least 5 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport.
The departure must be 13 miles from the airport before the arrival crosses the FAF.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-3-3. For the no-fix/procedure-turn version of the departure-arrival rule, when must the departure be established on a 45-degree-or-greater divergent course?
items.explanation: FIG 6-3-3 shows the procedure-turn variant. The departure must be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees before the arrival starts the procedure turn.

choices.text: Before the arrival starts the procedure turn.
Only after the arrival reports runway in sight.
Exactly 2 minutes after the arrival crosses the FAF.
After both aircraft pass the same NAVAID.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-1, FIG 6-4-2, and FIG 6-4-3. On same, converging, or crossing courses, the leading aircraft is at least 44 knots faster than the following aircraft. What longitudinal minimum may be used with DME/ATD?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-1, FIG 6-4-2, and FIG 6-4-3 illustrate the 44-knot speed-differential case. With DME/ATD, the applicable longitudinal minimum is 5 miles.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-4, FIG 6-4-5, and FIG 6-4-6. The leading aircraft is at least 22 knots faster than the following aircraft. What DME/ATD longitudinal spacing corresponds to that slower speed-differential case?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-4, FIG 6-4-5, and FIG 6-4-6 depict the 22-knot speed-differential case. The DME/ATD longitudinal minimum is 10 miles.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-7 and FIG 6-4-8. For DME/ATD separation while one aircraft climbs or descends through another aircraft's altitude, which geometry supports use of the 10-mile minimum?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-7 and FIG 6-4-8 show the DME/ATD altitude-through case. The 10-mile minimum applies when the descending aircraft is leading or the climbing aircraft is following.

choices.text: Only when both aircraft are level.
Only when the climbing aircraft is leading.
When the descending aircraft is leading or the climbing aircraft is following.
Whenever either aircraft reports visual contact.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-9 and FIG 6-4-10. Without DME/ATD, what timed separation may apply while one aircraft climbs or descends through another aircraft's altitude under the same-fix timing conditions?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-9 and FIG 6-4-10 illustrate the timed altitude-through alternative. The minimum is 5 minutes when the rule's altitude-difference and same-fix timing conditions are satisfied.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-9 and FIG 6-4-10. For the 5-minute timed altitude-through rule, how close in altitude must the two aircraft be when the altitude change starts?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-9 and FIG 6-4-10 support the timed altitude-through rule. One required condition is that the aircraft are separated by not more than 4,000 feet when the altitude change starts.

choices.text: Any altitude difference is allowed if time separation exists.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-19. For aircraft on opposite courses, what is the baseline timed window during which different altitudes are assigned before and after estimated passing?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-19 supports the opposite-course timing concept: assign different altitudes from 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after the aircraft are estimated to pass.

choices.text: 5 minutes before until 5 minutes after estimated passing.
10 minutes before until 10 minutes after estimated passing.
15 minutes before until crossing the same fix.
Only until both aircraft are established outbound.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-4-20. Which report supports discontinuing vertical separation for opposite-course aircraft?
items.explanation: FIG 6-4-20 illustrates one way to discontinue vertical separation on opposite courses: both aircraft report passing NAVAIDs, DME fixes, or waypoints that indicate they have passed each other.

choices.text: Both aircraft report passing fixes or waypoints indicating they have passed each other.
Only the leading aircraft reports established on course.
Either aircraft reports a 22-knot speed advantage.
The aircraft are within 4,000 feet vertically.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-5-1. Two aircraft are on radials from the same NAVAID that diverge by at least 15 degrees. What must be true before lateral separation exists?
items.explanation: FIG 6-5-1 illustrates diverging-radial lateral separation. Separation exists when either aircraft is clear of the airspace protected for the other aircraft.

choices.text: Either aircraft is clear of the airspace protected for the other aircraft.
Both aircraft are within 3 miles of the NAVAID.
Both aircraft report the same DME distance.
The radials diverge by exactly 10 degrees.

items.question_text: Use TBL 6-5-1. Without DME, what distance from the NAVAID corresponds to 30 degrees of divergence?
items.explanation: TBL 6-5-1 lists non-DME divergence distance minima. For 30 degrees of divergence, the distance is 8 NM from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: Use TBL 6-5-2. At FL180 through FL450, what DME divergence distance is required for 45 degrees of divergence?
items.explanation: TBL 6-5-2 gives the DME divergence distance minima. In the FL180 through FL450 column, 45 degrees of divergence requires 11 NM.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-5-2. Two aircraft are on different DME arcs about the same NAVAID. If the arcs are 35 NM or less from the NAVAID, what lateral spacing applies between arcs?
items.explanation: FIG 6-5-2 illustrates the different-DME-arc rule. For arcs 35 NM or less from the NAVAID, use 10 miles between the arcs.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-5-3. Between a DME arc and other protected airspace, what spacing applies when the arc is more than 35 NM from the NAVAID?
items.explanation: FIG 6-5-3 illustrates spacing between a DME arc and other protected airspace. When the arc is more than 35 NM from the NAVAID, use 10 miles.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-5-4. For direct courses based on NAVAIDs/radials at FL600 and below with course changes of 15 degrees or less, how is protected airspace built beyond 51 NM?
items.explanation: FIG 6-5-4 supports the direct-course protected-airspace rule: 4 miles each side out to 51 NM, then an increasing 4.5-degree angle to 10 miles each side at 130 NM.

choices.text: It remains fixed at 2 miles each side.
It remains fixed at 4 miles each side to 130 NM.
It increases on a 4.5-degree angle to 10 miles each side at 130 NM.
It immediately expands to 17 miles each side at the NAVAID.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-5-5. For a course change of 16 through 90 degrees, what overflown-side value applies from FL180 through FL230 inclusive?
items.explanation: FIG 6-5-5 illustrates the overflown-side minima for 16- through 90-degree course changes. From FL180 through FL230 inclusive, use 14 miles on the overflown side.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-5-6. For a course change of 91 through 180 degrees above FL230 through FL600, what overflown-side value applies?
items.explanation: FIG 6-5-6 illustrates the 91- through 180-degree course-change case. Above FL230 through FL600, use 34 miles on the overflown side.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-5-7. For RNAV lateral separation on a route no more than 8 miles wide, how far beyond the reference point must an aircraft be for protected airspace to no longer overlap?
items.explanation: FIG 6-5-7 supports RNAV lateral separation for routes no more than 8 miles wide. The required distance is 4 miles.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-5-8. For RNAV lateral separation on a route segment more than 8 miles wide, what distance is used unless the segment involves the extended usable-distance limitation beyond 130 miles?
items.explanation: FIG 6-5-8 illustrates RNAV lateral separation for wider route segments. Use 9 miles, except use 15 miles on route segments requiring the extended usable-distance limitation beyond 130 miles.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-7-1. In the timed ILS example, aircraft #3 is cleared to depart the LOM how long after aircraft #2 reports leaving the LOM inbound?
items.explanation: FIG 6-7-1 illustrates timed approach procedures using longitudinal separation only. Aircraft #3 is cleared to depart the LOM 2 minutes after aircraft #2 reports leaving the LOM inbound.

items.question_text: Use FIG 6-7-2. In the NDB timed approach example, why may aircraft #3 be descended to 2,000 feet after aircraft #2 departs the holding/approach fix inbound?
items.explanation: FIG 6-7-2 combines bearing, longitudinal, and vertical separation. Aircraft #2 has departed the holding/approach fix inbound and is leaving 2,000 feet at point A, allowing aircraft #3 to descend to 2,000 feet.

choices.text: Aircraft #2 has departed inbound and is leaving 2,000 feet at point A.
Aircraft #1 has cancelled IFR before the final approach fix.
Aircraft #3 is radar identified inside 1 mile.
Aircraft #2 and #3 are on diverging radials.
